name: Alterego
classification_notes: Alterego is a pixel-derived modular display typeface built from overlapping bitmap layers; its criss-cross stroke structure and grid-based construction place it firmly in the modular/pixel display genre.
summary: A pixel-grid display family by Pieter van Rosmalen, built from overlapping bitmap layers that can be individually coloured or animated to produce depth from a two-dimensional structure.
description: Originating in 2001 as a Photoshop experiment layering identical bitmap letters with varying transparency, Alterego was fully developed and released in 2018 with five weights and three Big display variants. The stroke pattern progresses from an open waffle grid in Thin to a dense closed square mesh in Bold, and because all styles share identical glyph widths, they are designed to be stacked, coloured independently or animated. Despite its experimental nature, the family carries a full Latin Extended character set with Vietnamese, Greek, Extended Cyrillic and Hebrew support.
foundry_blurb: Bold Monday describe Alterego as a pixel-and-modular display family rooted in van Rosmalen's long-standing fascination with bitmap type. The criss-cross of strokes moves across five weights from a thin waffle grid to a closed pattern of squares, with all fonts sharing glyph widths so they can be layered, individually coloured or animated. Three display styles (Alterego Big) were added alongside the main family, and a hearty variant with basic Latin coverage is offered free of charge.

name: Aniek
classification_notes: Aniek is a monolinear, non-connecting script drawn to evoke felt-tip handwriting, with a slight leftward slant distinguishing it from conventional right-leaning scripts. Its even rhythm and regularised proportions place it in the casual handwriting genre rather than formal calligraphic scripts.
summary: A seven-weight casual script by Pieter van Rosmalen, drawn from a former girlfriend's handwriting and distinguished by a slight leftward slant.
description: Monolinear strokes and rounded terminals give Aniek the character of writing with a soft felt-tip pen, while deliberate regularisation of proportions and spacing makes it legible enough for longer passages of body text. Unlike most script typefaces, it leans slightly to the left, lending it an unhurried, conversational quality. Seven weights from Light to Black cover Latin Extended, supporting the majority of European languages.
foundry_blurb: Bold Monday describe Aniek as an informal, friendly script inspired by the handwriting of a former girlfriend of its designer, Pieter van Rosmalen. The monolinear strokes and rounded terminals echo a soft felt-tip pen, and the typeface's slight leftward slant sets it apart from the majority of scripts on the market. Characters are deliberately not connected and proportions were harmonised to make the face suitable for extended text and small sizes.

name: Bilo
designer: Pieter van Rosmalen, Anna Khorash, Lora Shtirkova
classification_notes: A grotesque sans-serif with deliberate departures from convention, including egg-shaped rounds in heavier weights and steep italics that trace influence from Bodoni rather than traditional sans-serif models.
summary: A grotesque sans with egg-shaped rounds and unusually steep italics, rooted in Pieter van Rosmalen's early attempts to draw a low-contrast sans-serif version of Bodoni.
description: Van Rosmalen began exploring the forms in 2014, and the Bodoni black 'o' that started it all still shows in the oblong counters of heavier weights and the slightly concave stem terminals. Nine weights run from Hairline to Black, each with steep italics that lean further than is typical for a sans, and proportions shift from slender lights to wider, stubbier blacks. An extensive set of OpenType alternates, including a single-storey 'a' and multiple 'f' variants, lets the typeface move between grotesque and geometric registers without changing fonts.
foundry_blurb: Bold Monday describe Bilo as a grotesque in the literal sense, with Pieter van Rosmalen deliberately challenging common design practices that favour smooth curves and harmonious rounds. The typeface takes its name from the dog belonging to van Rosmalen's sister, and each style includes 18 dog-related icons designed by Dirk Uhlenbrock. The family spans 18 styles across 9 weights with extensive OpenType features and broad multilingual support.

name: Bilo Thai
classification_notes: A loopless Thai sans-serif companion to the Latin Bilo family, sharing Bilo's low-contrast geometric character derived from van Rosmalen's Bodoni experiment. The design is classified as a modern loopless sans with wide, circular forms.
summary: A Thai script companion to Bilo, designed by Promphan Suksumek alongside Pieter van Rosmalen's original Latin family, sharing its wide circular forms and pointy-eared terminals across nine weights.
description: Designed by Promphan 'Boom' Suksumek, this Thai extension of van Rosmalen's Bilo carries over the Latin's defining traits: low contrast, wide round shapes and the slightly pointy terminals that trace back to Bilo's origins as a sans-serif reinterpretation of Bodoni. Circular details echo native characteristics in letters such as Dochada and Thothan, while an alternate Sara-a glyph references Thai hand lettering conventions of the late 1950s and 60s. Stylistic alternates for Dodek, Totao and Ngongu allow a choice between rounder and more angular forms, giving the family flexibility across display and text sizes.
foundry_blurb: Bold Monday describe Bilo Thai as the Thai script companion to Pieter van Rosmalen's Bilo family, designed by Promphan 'Boom' Suksumek and released between 2018 and 2024. The modern loopless design shares Bilo's pointy-eared terminals and wide round shapes, which originated in van Rosmalen's experiment to draw a low-contrast sans-serif version of Bodoni. The family spans nine weights with stylistic alternates for select Thai characters and includes 18 dog-related icons per style, designed by Dirk Uhlenbrock.

name: Brando
classification_notes: Brando sits between humanist proportions and Egyptian slab forms, with lighter weights reading as open slab serifs and heavier weights introducing contrast. The foundry describes it as exploring the balance between mechanical and Egyptian forms, placing it firmly in the humanist slab category.
summary: A contemporary humanist slab serif by Mike Abbink and Aleksandra Samuļenkova that moves between Egyptian rigidity and fluid stroke work across eight weights from Hairline to Black.
description: Originally prompted by a bank logotype proposal, Brando developed into a full text and display family that balances mechanical structure with open, humanist letterforms. Lighter weights carry the character of an elegant slab serif while heavier cuts introduce contrast for comfortable text setting. The italics occupy a deliberate middle ground between true italic and oblique, and the family covers over forty languages across an extensive Latin and Cyrillic character set. It received a Judge's Choice Certificate of Typographic Excellence from the Type Directors Club in 2015.
foundry_blurb: Bold Monday describe Brando as a contemporary serif with humanist proportions that explores the balance between mechanical and Egyptian forms, originally inspired by a bank logotype proposal. The careful interaction of rigid and fluid strokes gives it modern appeal and sturdiness, with light styles reading as elegant slab serifs and heavier weights delivering even texture in text. The family spans eight weights from Hairline to Black with distinctive italics that strike a balance between true italic and oblique, and a comprehensive character set covering over forty languages.

name: Brando Arabic
designer: Khajag Apelian, Wael Morcos, Mike Abbink
classification_notes: Brando Arabic is an Arabic companion to the Brando slab serif, following Naskh script conventions while echoing the slab detailing of the Latin original through naturally occurring Arabic typographic features such as the Dal tail and Yeh stroke.
summary: An Arabic companion to Brando in eight weights, designed by Khajag Apelian and Wael Morcos to work alongside both Brando and Brando Sans across display and body copy settings.
description: Following Naskh script conventions, Brando Arabic mirrors the constructed-yet-organic character of Mike Abbink's Latin original by mapping slab detailing onto features inherent to the Arabic script, such as the Dal tail, the Hah's first stroke and the Kaf. Eight weights run from Hairline to Black, with increasing thick-thin contrast across the range, supporting Arabic, Persian and Urdu alongside an extensive Latin script coverage. The family won a Communication Arts Award of Excellence in 2018.
foundry_blurb: Bold Monday describe Brando Arabic as an interpretation of the Latin Brando's balance of hard and soft characteristics, translated through Naskh script conventions. The careful interaction of constructed and organic strokes gives it a modern appeal, visible particularly in the Heh medial, the Hah and the Kaf, while slab detailing is echoed through naturally occurring Arabic typographic features rather than applied directly.

name: Brando Sans
classification_notes: Described explicitly as a contemporary humanist sans by the foundry, with true italics, open apertures and idiosyncratic details like a curved lowercase l that place it firmly in the humanist tradition rather than geometric or grotesque camps.
summary: A humanist sans companion to the Brando serif family, available in eight weights with true italics and broad language coverage across Latin and Cyrillic scripts.
description: Designed by Mike Abbink and Aleksandra Samuļenkova between 2012 and 2020, Brando Sans pairs with the Brando serif while standing independently as a workhorse for editorial, branding and signage work. Its eight weights run from Hairline to Black, each with a true italic, and the character set extends to over 140 languages with small caps, multiple figure styles and fractions. Distinctive details such as the curved lowercase l and more symmetrical M and W give it a quiet individuality without undermining its utility.
foundry_blurb: Bold Monday describe Brando Sans as the distinct companion to Brando, sharing its contemporary humanist qualities but simplified for editorial design, branding, digital experiences and signage systems. They highlight its eight-weight range from Hairline to Black, coverage of over 140 languages, true italics and a comprehensive character set including small caps and multiple figure styles, noting that although simple at first sight it retains character and charm with unique traits of its own.

name: Buendia
classification_notes: Buendia deliberately spans grotesque, rounded, slab-serif and transitional styles within a single family, making it impossible to place in a single category; display is the best fit given its expressive range and primary use cases in packaging and advertising.
summary: A graduation project by César Puertas that builds six formally distinct styles — grotesque, rounded, slab-serif and transitional — from a single shared skeleton.
description: Rather than progressing through weights alone, Buendia varies finishing across its six members: a thin and medium sans, a roman and italic serif, a bold slab and an extra beefy rounded sans. All share closed apertures, swashy tails and slightly curved diagonals, which lend the family a consistent warmth across otherwise contrasting styles. Suited to packaging, advertising and editorial work where a designer needs tonal variety without switching families.
foundry_blurb: Bold Monday describe Buendia as an exploration of what a type family can be beyond traditional weight and width progressions. César Puertas structured the family into six styles with distinct but matching flavours — grotesque, rounded, slab-serif and transitional — all drawn from the same skeleton but with different finishing, providing a compact yet flexible set of options for packaging, advertising, books and editorial design.

name: Capibara
classification_notes: Capibara is built exclusively from horizontal and vertical strokes with no curves, placing it firmly in the geometric modular tradition. Its Constructivist references and strictly rectilinear construction make it a display typeface despite the availability of a monospaced variant.
summary: A geometric modular typeface built entirely from horizontal and vertical strokes, referencing Constructivist typographic experiments of the early 20th century.
description: Designed by Pieter van Rosmalen between 2002 and 2008, Capibara draws on the experiments of artists like Theo van Doesburg and H.Th. Wijdeveld, constructing every letterform from orthogonal strokes alone. Slightly rounded corners soften the inherent rigidity, and the letterforms deliberately avoid strict grid adherence to prevent the coldness common in modular designs. Ten styles span proportional and monospaced variants across five weights, with small caps, multiple numeral sets and broad Latin coverage included.
foundry_blurb: Bold Monday describe Capibara as a geometric typeface family solely constructed of horizontal and vertical strokes with no curves, echoing Constructivist typographic experiments of the early 20th century. They note that rounded corners and a deliberate looseness from the grid help Capibara avoid the cold harshness typical of modular designs. The family is named after designer Pieter van Rosmalen's favourite animal, the capybara.

name: Dico
designer: Pieter van Rosmalen, Diana Ovezea, Jacques Le Bailly
classification_notes: The core family is a compact, open sans-serif with warm, friendly letterforms and swinging italics, characteristics consistent with humanist sans design. The suite expands into slab, monospaced and script sub-families, but the proportional sans forms the structural and tonal centre.
summary: A suite of eight sub-families by Pieter van Rosmalen, built around a compact humanist sans originally drawn in 2004 for Teldesign and expanded to 45 fonts spanning slab, monospaced, code and typewriter variants.
description: Compact proportions and open, restrained letterforms define the core sans, which gains warmth through its swinging italics and a rounded Soft variant. The family extends into proportional and monospaced slab cuts, two Code variants with swapped defaults and alternative letterforms for coding clarity, and a Typewriter subfamily offering roughened mono versions of the Sans, Slab and Script. Monospaced script styles loosely based on the Corinthian Script for the IBM Selectric add a further, more playful register to the system.
foundry_blurb: Bold Monday describe Dico — from the Latin for 'I speak, I give a speech' — as a varied suite of eight proportional and monospaced sub-families circling a sans van Rosmalen began in 2004 for Teldesign. The family is defined by compact proportions and open yet restrained letterforms that warm up in the italics; monospaced script styles loosely based on the Corinthian Script for the IBM Selectric push things further still. The 45-font system includes Sans, Slab, Soft, Mono, Mono Slab, Code One, Code Two and Typewriter, with OpenType alternates for dots, fractions and individual letterforms throughout.

name: Feisar
classification_notes: A display typeface derived from a 12-pixel bitmap UI font, drawing on science fiction, video gaming and techno culture aesthetics — firmly in the display category with a techno/pixel subcategory.
summary: An outline typeface evolved from a 12-pixel bitmap UI font, blending 1970s sci-fi, 1980s gaming and 1990s techno aesthetics into a recognisable display face.
description: Originally designed as a pixel-grid bitmap font for a game interface, Feisar has been redrawn as a scalable outline family with five weights from ExtraLight to Bold. Paul van der Laan reinterpreted the pixel-grid constraints rather than simply tracing them, which gives the face its particular legibility at display sizes despite its retro-techno character. Best suited to titles, gaming interfaces and projects that need a deliberately period-coded digital flavour.
foundry_blurb: Bold Monday describe Feisar as a display typeface that marries the aesthetics of 1970s science fiction, 1980s video gaming and 1990s techno culture. The family originated as a 12-pixel bitmap font for a user interface, and the current outline version reinterprets many details to overcome the limitations of the pixel grid, remaining expressive and recognisable while retaining the legibility of its bitmap origins.

name: Feisar Express
classification_notes: Feisar Express is a connecting inline script designed for expressive display use, with a retro-futuristic and technical character that places it firmly in the display script category.
summary: A retro-futuristic inline connecting script by Paul van der Laan, available in six style variants built around different connection paths between letters.
description: Designed by Paul van der Laan as an offshoot of the Feisar family, this inline script was conceived as an exploration of what current font technology makes possible. The family splits into two inline styles, One and Two, each offered in three connection variants: Uptown (top-connecting), Downtown (bottom-connecting) and Crosstown (alternating). The result is a display typeface with a distinctly technical, electro-mechanical edge suited to headline work in music, tech and entertainment contexts.
foundry_blurb: Bold Monday describe Feisar Express as a retro-futuristic inline script for expressive display typography with a technical touch, related to the broader Feisar family and begun as an exploration of current font technology. It comes in two connecting inline styles, One and Two, each with three variants for top, bottom and alternating connections: Uptown, Downtown and Crosstown.

name: Flex
classification_notes: Flex has open letterforms, true italics, small caps and oldstyle figures suggesting a humanist sans-serif orientation. Its economic proportions and legibility at small sizes align with the humanist sans tradition.
summary: A humanist sans by Paul van der Laan, originally conceived in 1999 as a multi-purpose family with strong small-size legibility and wayfinding credentials.
description: Designed with open letterforms and economic proportions, Flex performs at small text sizes as readily as it does in bold headlines and signage contexts. Five weights, each with a true italic, are accompanied by small caps, oldstyle figures and ligatures, giving the family the typographic tools expected of a refined text face. Its space-saving proportions make it particularly well suited to wayfinding systems where positive and negative applications must both hold up.
foundry_blurb: Bold Monday describe Flex as a true multi-purpose typeface conceived in 1999 by Paul van der Laan, with open letterforms for strong small-size readability, true italics, small caps, oldstyle figures and ligatures. They note its economic proportions suit both large display use and signage or wayfinding systems where text must work in both positive and negative contexts.

name: Goldich
classification_notes: Goldich features sharply cut, angular letterforms with thin parts deliberately weathered away into stencil-like shapes, making it a display serif designed primarily for large-size settings and all-caps use.
summary: A sharply angular display serif by Jasper de Waard, its letterforms shaped by a weathering metaphor that produces stencil-like cuts best seen at large sizes.
description: Designed over several years by Jasper de Waard and named after a geological classification of mineral weathering, the family draws on that idea to create characters whose thin strokes appear worn away into precise, crystalline angles. Five weights from Regular to Black each come with italics, and the italics introduce their own elegant quality for mixed-case text. OpenType features cover alternates for a, g and the ampersand, alongside a full range of numeral styles and fractions, with coverage across more than 100 Latin-script languages. It received a Type Directors Club Certificate of Typographic Excellence in 2022.
foundry_blurb: Bold Monday describe Goldich as a family whose letterforms have crystallised into sharply cut shapes, inspired by the Goldich series in geology, which predicts the weathering rate of minerals. Designer Jasper de Waard worked on the family over several years, producing five weights plus italics whose stencil-like characters shine in large-size and all-caps settings, while the italics add elegance to body text. The fonts cover over 100 languages and include a full set of OpenType features for refined typography.

name: Jurriaan 3D
classification_notes: A strictly angular, geometric display face built around perspective and three-dimensional illusion across four layered style variants; unambiguously a display typeface designed for large-scale applications.
summary: A four-variant layered display face by Pieter van Rosmalen that applies drop shadows, hatching and ribbed shading to compact angular letterforms rooted in Jurriaan Schrofer's 1969 geometry.
description: Combining Schrofer's 1969 typographic experiments with van Rosmalen's own Capibara, the typeface explores three-dimensional illusion across four styles: Dark fills surrounding space with a severe drop shadow, Ribbed breaks the shade into five lined layers, Shaded mixes hatching with hard shadow for tonal variation, and Fill acts as a base layer for colour combinations with the others. Despite its obvious affinity for poster-scale work, it carries a full OpenType feature set including proper fractions, ordinals, superscripts and inferiors, with wide Latin language coverage.
foundry_blurb: Bold Monday describe Jurriaan 3D as a crossbreed between a typeface designed by Jurriaan Schrofer in 1969 and van Rosmalen's Capibara from 2002, exploring perspective and dimensionality in a flat medium through four design variants of compact, strictly angular letterforms. They note that while the family is at home in large-size applications, it also supports refined micro typography through comprehensive OpenType features and broad language support.

name: Kristal
classification_notes: Kristal is rooted in calligraphic tradition with acute, blade-like stroke endings that give it an expressive humanist character; the Open Caps inline titling style and spirited italics reinforce a calligraphically informed, display-capable serif.
summary: A book serif by Eyal Holtzman with blade-like stroke endings and spirited italics, extended by an all-caps inline titling style and an Ornaments font.
description: Originating as a logotype sketch, Kristal grew into a family of eight styles across three weights, each with a distinctive italic whose acute terminals enliven extended text. The Open Caps style introduces an inline treatment suited to monumental lettering, covers and card designs, harmonising with the text weights while functioning independently. The family received a Type Directors Club Certificate of Typographic Excellence in 2022 and covers an extensive range of Latin-script languages.
foundry_blurb: Bold Monday describe Kristal as a versatile book face that delivers a pleasant reading experience with a special character and sparkle, thanks to its almost blade-like acute stroke endings. They note it is headstrong and expressive yet rooted in calligraphy, easy to combine with other typefaces, while the Open Caps display style evokes a timeless, festive look suitable for monumental lettering and elegant cover designs.

name: Logical
classification_notes: Described as having a DIN-like technical structure paired with humanist warmth; oval, compact letterforms with open apertures and tapered terminals place it firmly in the humanist sans tradition.
summary: A humanist sans by Edgar Walthert built from a 13 px grid for screen rendering, with eight weights, true italics and an integrated icon system accessible via OpenType features.
description: Drawn on a 13 px grid to optimise rendering on standard-resolution displays, Logical combines a compact oval structure with open apertures and softly tapered terminals for warmth at screen sizes. Eight weights from Thin to Black each come with a true italic, and the family includes an extensive set of pictograms accessible by typing keywords between colons or via Stylistic Sets for English, German, Dutch and French. Smart underline features for interactive use cases and a broad Latin language set make it a practical choice for UI, signage and web typography.
foundry_blurb: Bold Monday describe Logical as a sans-serif family built on strong underlying principles and repeating design elements, tempered by affable, soothing details that result in a warm and approachable design. Designer Edgar Walthert started by drawing on a 13 px grid to improve screen rendering, and the family includes smart icon features that convert keywords into symbols, alongside special underlined glyph variants for links in interactive environments.

name: Macula
classification_notes: Macula is a display typeface built around the visual concept of impossible objects, with five distinct styles designed to be layered for multi-colour effects. Its primary purpose is decorative and illusionistic rather than functional text setting.
summary: A layering display typeface by Jacques Le Bailly built on the visual logic of impossible objects, with five styles that stack to produce multi-colour three-dimensional letterforms.
description: Designed by Jacques Le Bailly in 2012, the five styles — Line, Solid, Background, Shaded and Shading — can be used individually or stacked in layers to create chromatic, illusionistic typography. Each character is drawn in two alternating variants, cycled automatically via OpenType contextual alternates to keep the rhythm of the letterforms visually lively. Stylistic alternates for A, E, N and O add further variation. The concept draws on the impossible-object tradition explored by Oscar Reutersvärd in the 1930s and made widely known through M.C. Escher.
foundry_blurb: Bold Monday describe Macula as the 'impossible typeface', its design rooted in the concept of impossible objects as explored by Swedish artist Oscar Reutersvärd and Dutch artist M.C. Escher. The five styles can be used independently or layered on top of each other to create multi-coloured typographic effects, with automatic OpenType alternation between two character variants to maintain typographic liveliness.

name: Mala
classification_notes: Mala is described as having modern proportions with a hint of typewriter-feel, suggesting a humanist sans with idiosyncratic, slightly quaint character rather than a strict geometric or grotesque approach.
summary: A 32-style sans designed by Barbara Bigosińska with cartography and information labelling in mind, spanning three widths with swash capitals and rich OpenType features.
description: Developed from 2013 to 2016 via the Type and Media course at KABK in The Hague, Mala spans Normal, Condensed and Extended widths across eight weights, with italics in the normal width. The family is built around the specific demands of maps and layered data — where type must distinguish, label and structure rather than read continuously. Two sets of decorative swash capitals, small caps, extensive ligatures, ornaments, arrows and multiple numeral sets make it unusually well-equipped for complex typographic layouts.
foundry_blurb: Bold Monday describe Mala as designed for maps and other complex, multilayered sources of information, where type is used very differently from continuous reading. They note its 32 styles across three widths serve everything from headlines to labels to extreme display use, and characterise its voice as a quaint typeface of modern proportions with a hint of typewriter-feel.

name: Merwede
classification_notes: Merwede draws on early Renaissance typefaces and the work of Fournier, Gill, Van Krimpen and Majoor, giving it a humanist character rooted in calligraphic tradition and book typography rather than strict historical revival.
summary: A humanist text serif by book designer Titus Schulz, distinguished by unconventional details including upright italic capitals and a full set of musical chord symbols.
description: Designed by Titus Schulz, a book designer with 25 years of experience, Merwede is rooted in calligraphic practice and draws loose inspiration from early Renaissance type and designers including Fournier, Gill and Van Krimpen without reviving any single model. The family runs across four weights — Regular, Firm, Medium and Bold — each with italics, where Firm sits between Regular and Medium and is intended for posters, reversed-out text and inscriptions. A full set of musical chord and notation symbols addresses a longstanding gap Schulz identified across existing book typefaces.
foundry_blurb: Bold Monday describe Merwede as a charming, multi-purpose text family designed by Titus Schulz, whose 25 years as a book designer and calligraphic training at ArtEZ Academy of Arts Arnhem informed its unconventional details. They note that references span early Renaissance typefaces and the work of Fournier, Gill, Van Krimpen, Salden and Majoor, and highlight the inclusion of a unique set of musical chord symbols that Schulz felt were poorly handled in almost all existing typefaces.

name: Nitti
classification_notes: Nitti is explicitly monospaced and rooted in 19th-century Grotesque sans-serif traditions, making it a grotesque monospaced typeface. Its idiosyncratic letterforms draw directly from early English sans-serif designs.
summary: A monospaced grotesque in five weights by Pieter van Rosmalen and Yanek Iontef, drawing on the idiosyncratic letterforms of early 19th-century British sans-serifs.
description: Rooted in the quirky, humanist character of Victorian-era Grotesques, Nitti brings warmth and personality to a fixed-width format across ten styles from Light to Black with matching italics. The character set is unusually broad, covering Latin, Cyrillic, Greek and Hebrew scripts and supporting well over a hundred languages. It sits within a wider Grotesque-themed collection from Bold Monday that includes the poster-weight Stanley and the proportional Nitti Grotesk.
foundry_blurb: Bold Monday describe Nitti as a monospaced typeface family in five weights rooted in early 19th-century Grotesque sans-serif designs, originally a British invention that spread across Europe and 20th-century America. Named after Frank 'The Enforcer' Nitti, Al Capone's enforcer, it forms part of a broader Grotesque-inspired collection alongside Stanley and Nitti Grotesk, and features an extensive character set covering Latin, Greek, Cyrillic and Hebrew.

name: Nitti Grotesk
classification_notes: Directly descended from early English grotesque sans-serifs, van Rosmalen leaned into the quirky, idiosyncratic letterforms of that tradition to give the proportional family warmth and humanity typical of the genre.
summary: A proportional grotesque by Pieter van Rosmalen, grown from the same lineage as the monospaced Nitti and drawing on the idiosyncratic quirks of early English sans-serifs.
description: Born from a request by Information Architects for a proportional companion to Nitti, the family took three attempts before van Rosmalen settled on pushing the design further into grotesque territory where Nitti had strayed from convention. Seven text weights with italics sit alongside five display-only weights, with a condensed sibling available for headline use. The extended Latin coverage and range of OpenType features make it a thorough workhorse for editorial and identity work.
foundry_blurb: Bold Monday describe Nitti Grotesk as the proportional companion to Nitti, part of a broader collection of grotesque-inspired typefaces by Pieter van Rosmalen that traces back to his sans-serif Capone. The family incorporates the peculiarities of early English grotesques to deliver humanity and warmth, offered in seven text weights plus italics and five display weights, with a condensed variant for punchy headlines.

name: Nitti Grotesk Condensed
classification_notes: A condensed grotesque sans with squarish, oval forms derived from the wider Nitti Grotesk family, designed for display and headline use with an urgent, restrained character.
summary: A condensed companion to Nitti Grotesk by Pieter van Rosmalen, built across twelve weights for headlines, navigation and advertising.
description: Where Nitti Grotesk favours generous, rounded proportions, this condensed variant trades those curves for squarish, oval forms that read as tighter and more urgent. Twelve weights run from Hairline to Ultra, with italics covering Light through Black, giving editorial and corporate designers a wide palette for high-impact display work. It pairs naturally with Nitti Grotesk but holds its own alongside other typefaces in condensed-column or breaking-news contexts.
foundry_blurb: Bold Monday describe Nitti Grotesk Condensed as the space-saving, emphatic companion to the Nitti Grotesk family, a variant that designer Pieter van Rosmalen had always intended to draw alongside his generously proportioned original. The condensed cut introduces squarish, boxy forms with narrow oval rounds for a more restrained and urgent feel, suited to headlines, advertising and navigation. The family runs to twelve weights and includes italics across the core weights.

name: Nitti Mostro
designer: Pieter van Rosmalen, Aleksandra Samuļenkova, Yanek Iontef
classification_notes: Derived from the heaviest weights of Nitti Grotesk and designed explicitly for display use with layerable styles including Solid, Shadow, Inline, Gradient, Chrome, Disco and Stencil variants; sits firmly in the display category as a decorative, effect-driven grotesque family.
summary: An eighteen-style layerable display grotesque spun off from Nitti Grotesk, built for posters, signage and screen effects with sub-families covering Comic, Disco and Stencil treatments.
description: Built on the heaviest weights of Nitti Grotesk, this family strips ascenders and descenders short and tightens letter fit to maximise impact at large sizes. The eighteen styles are organised into four sub-families — Mostro, Comic, Disco and Stencil — each designed to layer together for colour, shading and animation effects. Nine monster pictograms drawn by Dirk Uhlenbrock round out the package, fitting it squarely in the 'Italo-American Grotesque' lineage Bold Monday has built around the Nitti series.
foundry_blurb: Bold Monday describe Nitti Mostro as the boldest and most fun entry in the Nitti series, calling it beefy, dazzling, flashy and rough. They position it as a display-focused extension of Nitti Grotesk, part of their self-described 'Italo-American Grotesques' collection alongside Stanley, Nitti, Nitti Typewriter and Nitti Grotesk, with all styles designed to be layered for colourful and animated effects.

name: Nitti Typewriter
classification_notes: A monospaced typeface rooted in 19th-century Grotesque sans-serif design, deliberately styled to evoke typewriter aesthetics through five distinct visual treatments including corrected text and underlined variants.
summary: A monospaced typeface from Bold Monday that channels typewriter aesthetics through five stylistic variants, each evoking a different aspect of mechanical type.
description: Built on the foundation of Nitti, this family extends the grotesque-rooted monospaced design into overtly typewriter-inspired territory. The five variants — Normal, Open, Underlined, Corrected and Cameo — each reference a distinct typewriter convention, from ink strike-throughs to correction marks. Character coverage spans Latin, Greek, Cyrillic and Hebrew, supporting an exceptionally broad range of European and Asian languages.
foundry_blurb: Bold Monday describe Nitti Typewriter as a playful nod to typewriter aesthetics, available in five flavours: Normal, Open, Underlined, Corrected and Cameo. The family traces its origins to the first sans-serif designs of the 19th century — the Grotesques — and is named, like the rest of the Nitti series, after Frank 'The Enforcer' Nitti of Al Capone's gang. It features an extensive character set covering Latin, Greek, Cyrillic and Hebrew scripts.

name: Oskar
classification_notes: An all-caps display type series drawing on early 20th-century Dutch architectural and commercial lettering traditions, blending geometric sans structure with Art Deco characteristics. Its origins in sign painting and lettering manuals rather than printing type distinguish it from conventional geometric sans-serifs.
summary: An all-caps display series by Paul van der Laan rooted in early 20th-century Dutch architectural and sign-painted lettering, offered in two stylistic flavours each with inline variants.
description: Originally drawn in 2002 for the restoration lettering of a Jan Duiker school building in The Hague, Oskar has since grown into twelve styles across two subfamilies. Oskar One retains the pointy apices and expressive irregularity of 1920s Dutch lettering manuals, while Oskar Two pulls the forms closer to classical geometric sans whilst preserving an Art Deco sensibility. Each weight in both subfamilies is paired with a manually hinted inline variant suited to façade work, titling and screen use alike.
foundry_blurb: Bold Monday describe Oskar as an all-caps type series inspired by Dutch architectural and commercial letterings of the early 20th century, particularly those painted on walls and shopfronts or executed in metal. They note that this style was cultivated by sign painters, draftsmen and architects and passed down through lettering manuals rather than printing type. The family comes in two flavours — One and Two — each available in three weights with accompanying inline styles, and with carefully drawn, manually hinted inline variants suited to both screen and façade applications.

name: Panno Sign
classification_notes: Designed for wayfinding and signage with narrow proportions, large x-height, and open counters; minimal stroke contrast places it in the humanist sans tradition, optimised for legibility at distance.
summary: A signage-optimised sans by Pieter van Rosmalen, originally developed for romanised street name signs in South Korea.
description: Designed between 2008 and 2010 to handle tri-script Korean street signs where Latin, Korean and Chinese characters share limited space, Panno Sign uses narrow proportions, an oversized x-height and open counters to maintain legibility under compression. The family comes in Normal and Rounded variants, each offered in Positive and Negative cuts to maintain consistent optical weight across light-on-dark and dark-on-light applications. An extended Latin character set covers a wide range of languages, with OpenType alternates for 'a', 'g' and the ampersand.
foundry_blurb: Bold Monday describe Panno Sign as optimised for signage and developed specifically for the romanisation of street names in South Korea, where signs must accommodate Korean, Chinese and Latin scripts simultaneously. The design addresses space constraints through narrow proportions, a large x-height and open counters, while avoiding the cold, constructed feel associated with traditional wayfinding typefaces such as DIN.

name: Panno Text
classification_notes: Narrow proportions, large x-height, open counters and minimal stroke contrast place it firmly in the humanist sans tradition, with functional clarity akin to DIN but warmer in character.
summary: A text-optimised reworking of Panno Sign by Pieter van Rosmalen, with narrow proportions, a large x-height and true italics across six weights.
description: Derived from Panno Sign, this family adjusts letterforms and completely reworks the spacing to hold up at small sizes in continuous text. The narrow proportions and open counters make it practical for editorial, exhibition and corporate environments where DIN-style constructed sans-serifs feel too cold. Six weights from Light to Bold each come with matching true italics, and OpenType features cover fractions, multiple numeral styles and stylistic alternates for 'a', 'g', 'y' and the ampersand.
foundry_blurb: Bold Monday describe Panno Text as a reworking and extension of Panno Sign, optimised for continuous text. They highlight its narrow proportions, large x-height and open counters, noting that it brings optimal functionality with a bit of fresh, warm air to applications where typefaces like DIN may feel too constructed or cold, and that it adapts well across editorial, corporate, exhibition and manual contexts.

name: Pilot
classification_notes: Pilot is a condensed informal display face with angular, handmade qualities reminiscent of sign painter lettering, placing it firmly in the display category with a condensed subcategory rooted in expressive jobbing type traditions.
summary: A condensed informal display family with angular, sign-painter qualities, designed by Aleksandra Samuļenkova at the KABK and released across five weights with italics and small caps.
description: Designed from 2012 to 2017, Pilot was developed as a condensed jobbing face suited to headlines, posters, book jackets and packaging. Its angular letterforms and handmade character lend it a nostalgic warmth, particularly evident in the bold italics where sign-painter influences come through most strongly. The family includes two variants of small caps that mimic traditional catchwords and logotypes, and it remains one of the few contemporary typefaces available as metal type, winning first prize at the Fine Press Book Association's inaugural Student Type Design Competition in 2013.
foundry_blurb: Bold Monday describe Pilot as condensed by design, an informal jobbing typeface for short texts and display use including headlines, posters, book jackets and packaging. The angular design and handmade character give it a slightly nostalgic feel reminiscent of sign painter lettering, especially in the bold italics. The family of ten styles was developed by Latvian-born Aleksandra Samuļenkova during her Type & Media studies at the KABK, and it won a TDC Certificate of Typographic Excellence in 2017.

name: Pim
classification_notes: Pim is a display typeface rooted in Dutch mid-20th century glass advertising lettering, with wide round forms, monospaced construction, ball terminals and an inline variant. Its origin in letterpress printing and its decorative feature set place it firmly in the display category.
summary: A display typeface rooted in Dutch mid-20th century glass shop-window lettering, with monospaced square construction, ball terminals and an elaborate set of swashes, ornaments and pictograms.
description: Originally designed for letterpress printing using polymer and wood type, Pim digitises the wide, round aesthetic of Dutch 'Pootjesglas' etalage lettering associated with Johannes Willem 'Pim' Pootjes. All glyphs sit on a square grid with an extremely high x-height and no descenders, allowing very tight setting with no leading. Three weights plus an inline style cover extended Latin and Cyrillic, while clip-on swashes, Art Deco alternates, manicules, emoji and goggle-eyed umlauts extend the repertoire well beyond the original analogue capitals.
foundry_blurb: Bold Monday describe Pim as originating in an analogue letterpress project by Pieter van Rosmalen, who produced the fonts in polymer and wood with every monospaced letter cut as a square cube. Inspired by the iconic 'Pootjesglas' shop-window lettering of mid-20th century Dutch advertising boards, digital Pim expands that capital-only origin into a full character set including lowercase, alternates, pictograms, ornaments, borders, display numerals, swash connections and Cyrillic support with Bulgarian and Serbian alternates.

name: Puffin
designer: Pieter van Rosmalen, Sabina Chipară, Jacques Le Bailly
classification_notes: Described explicitly as a humanist sans with generous spacing and oblique italics suited to screen and UI use; the Display variants add curved, expressive letterforms but the base family sits firmly in the humanist sans tradition.
summary: A humanist sans family by Pieter van Rosmalen that spans a workhorse text cut and two expressive display variants, grown from a single bold display weight released in 2008.
description: The base Puffin is a practical humanist sans with generous spacing, a 15-degree oblique italic and disambiguated forms for capital I, lowercase l and the numeral 1, making it well-suited to UI and on-screen reading. Puffin Display and Puffin Display Soft increase the x-height, tighten spacing and introduce more expressive curved letterforms and rounded terminals for medium to large sizes. Across three subfamilies, the family runs to 42 styles, with extensive Latin language coverage stretching to over 100 languages.
foundry_blurb: Bold Monday describe Puffin as the grown-up evolution of Pieter van Rosmalen's earlier single-weight display sans Pinup, expanded over more than a decade into two comprehensive type series. The plain Puffin is the most restrained member, suited to on-screen reading and UI design, while Puffin Display and Puffin Display Soft offer more flavourful, curved letterforms and swinging true italics for personable applications at larger sizes. A companion family, Puffin Arcade, extends the range into playful territory for posters and video games.

name: Puffin Arcade
classification_notes: A family of 12 bitmapped styles derived from Puffin Display Soft's heaviest weight, each with a distinct pixel-art or arcade-themed design treatment. Classified as display due to its decorative, layerable bitmap construction intended for expressive and animated use.
summary: A family of 12 bitmapped display styles derived from Puffin Display Soft, designed for layering and multi-colour animation.
description: Each of the 12 styles carries a distinct theme, from Dither and Liquid to Warp, Yoko and Tate, all built on the same heavy bitmap skeleton so that most styles stack cleanly on top of one another. The layering system was conceived for flashing, blinking and running animations, making the family particularly suited to kinetic screen work and title sequences. It sits comfortably alongside the calmer Puffin and Puffin Display families when a project calls for both registers.
foundry_blurb: Bold Monday describe Puffin Arcade as an entertaining family of 12 bitmapped fonts based on the heaviest weight of Puffin Display Soft, each with a different design theme ranging from Dither and Nerf to Liquid, Warp, Wipe, Yoko, Tate, Level and Foozle. All styles except Foozle can be layered for multi-colour animations, and the family was designed with Python assistance by Frederik Berlaen under Pieter van Rosmalen's direction between 2012 and 2021.

name: Quinn
classification_notes: Quinn draws explicitly on realist (transitional) serif models such as Johann Fleischmann's work, with high stroke contrast and vertical stress, stopping short of full Didone rigidity. The family sits squarely in the transitional tradition, updated for contemporary publishing use.
summary: A two-subfamily serif by Diana Ovezea, split into Text and Display optical sizes, drawn in the tradition of Fleischmann's realist serifs with high stroke contrast and pronounced ink-traps.
description: Developed between 2013 and 2019 beginning at the Type and Media course at KABK in The Hague, Quinn comprises 24 styles across Quinn Text (10 styles, optimised for 6–24pt) and Quinn Display (14 styles, for sizes above 24pt). Display styles carry slightly shorter ascenders and descenders to enable tighter, more impactful settings, while top serifs align with the x-height for strong horizontal rhythm. A thorough OpenType feature set including small caps, multiple numeral sets, ligatures and ordinals makes it well suited to complex publication hierarchies.
foundry_blurb: Bold Monday describe Quinn as a typeface family for editorial design and publishing of all kinds, with a reliable and resolute constitution suited particularly to textbooks, non-fiction and catalogues, while carrying enough flavour for branding and advertising. They note that its overall theme takes cues from realist (transitional) serif faces such as those of Johann Fleischmann, but that it stands firmly in the modern age with high stroke contrast and vertical emphasis, without the rigidity of a Didone.

name: Rekall
classification_notes: Rekall is an aggressive, compressed display family built on futuristic stencil logic rather than conventional letterform construction, making it firmly a display typeface with no body-text application intended.
summary: A futuristic stencil display family by Shiva Nallaperumal, built around an aggressive compressed logic that prioritises visual impact over legibility.
description: Originating as a logotype for a high-fashion streetwear brand, the design treats stencil cuts as a structural principle rather than an afterthought, producing spiky compressed letterforms that grow into chunky blocks at heavier weights. Consistent white space and x-height across all five weights allow mixed-style settings within a single word. Five decorated variants in Rekall Two, including the outline Factory and the striped Techno, extend the palette for posters, record sleeves and apparel graphics.
foundry_blurb: Bold Monday describe Rekall as a forceful display family that puts decipherability to the test. Designed by Shiva Nallaperumal starting from a streetwear logotype brief, the family uses futuristic stencil elements as its underlying structural logic rather than cutting into pre-existing letterforms. The consistent x-height and internal spacing across weights make animated multi-style settings possible, while five decorated styles including Factory, Infra, Lazer, Machine and Techno push the family toward poster, sleeve and board applications.

name: Rigby
classification_notes: A sans-serif with angular terminals and personable letterforms including a distinctive double-storey g and e, rooted in a custom typeface for a Dutch public broadcaster; its warmth and character place it firmly in the humanist sans tradition.
summary: A deliberately compact two-weight sans-serif by Pieter van Rosmalen, derived from a custom typeface made for Dutch public broadcaster NTR, with a monospaced sibling and a RIBBI structure.
description: Designed by Pieter van Rosmalen and released between 2023 and 2025, Rigby offers Regular and Bold with matching italics, kept intentionally simple at a time when many families chase weight counts and variable axes. Its default letterforms include a distinctive double-storey g and a two-storey e with a curved bar, both of which can be swapped out via stylistic sets for more neutral alternatives. The monospaced variant shares the same character and warmth, making the family useful across editorial, UI and coding contexts where a small, coherent system is preferable to a sprawling one.
foundry_blurb: Bold Monday position Rigby as a refreshingly uncomplicated sans-serif in two weights with italics and a monospaced sibling, designed by Pieter van Rosmalen as a retail version of a custom typeface for NTR, a Dutch public broadcaster. They highlight its personable default letterforms, rich contextual alternates and stylistic sets for toning down its character, and note it covers 40 Latin-based languages.

name: Stanley
classification_notes: Rooted in 19th-century British Grotesques and early American Gothics, Stanley is explicitly poster-oriented and bold, placing it in the display category with a grotesque subcategory lineage.
summary: A broad-shouldered poster grotesque in three styles, drawing on 19th-century British sans-serifs and early American Gothic traditions.
description: Designed by Pieter van Rosmalen and released in 2008, Stanley comes in Normal, Stencil and Stencil Rough cuts, all sharing the quirky, idiosyncratic character shapes typical of early English Grotesques. The family covers Latin, Greek and Cyrillic scripts with an extensive language list and includes arrows, dingbats and OpenType extras such as ligatures and stylistic alternates. Bold Monday position it within a broader 'Italo-American Grotesque' collection alongside Nitti Grotesk and Nitti Typewriter.
foundry_blurb: Bold Monday describe Stanley as a bold, broad-shouldered poster typeface collection rooted in the first sans-serif designs of the 19th century — the Grotesques. Originally a British invention, these forms spread across mainland Europe and early 20th-century USA where they were known as Gothics. The quirky, idiosyncratic shapes of these early English sans-serifs give them the humanity and warmth still appreciated by graphic designers today, and Stanley is part of Bold Monday's wider 'Italo-American Grotesque' collection.

name: Trio Grotesk
classification_notes: A revival of Kaart Antieke, an early 20th-century sans-serif associated with Dutch modernism; the ink-spread characteristics of letterpress printing preserved in the forms place it firmly in the historical grotesque tradition.
summary: A revival of Kaart Antieke, the early 20th-century sans-serif found in Piet Zwart's unpublished essay on modern typography, preserving the ink-spread character of the original letterpress printing.
description: Florian Schick began this as a student project at KABK Type & Media after discovering the only two surviving copies of Piet Zwart's essay 'Van oude tot nieuwe typografie' at the Meermanno Museum in Den Haag. Working from the original 7 pt printed text, he replicated the printed image with care, retaining the rounded stroke endings and junctions caused by ink spread and pressure rather than normalising them away. The family offers three weights with italics, a broad Latin-extended character set, and OpenType features including multiple numeral sets, arrows, ornaments and dingbats.
foundry_blurb: Bold Monday describe Trio Grotesk as Florian Schick's personal interpretation of Kaart Antieke, the typeface used in Piet Zwart's famous but never officially published essay on modern typography. Schick discovered the booklet during a visit to the Meermanno Museum and chose to revive it faithfully, preserving letterpress-specific features such as rounded stroke endings caused by ink spread. The family comes in three weights plus italics with a Latin-extended character set and broad OpenType support.
